exemplar.title: The Relationship Between Glass Thickness and UV Light Intensity Reduction

detailedExemplarData.title: The Relationship Between Glass Thickness and UV Light Intensity Reduction

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs include clear headings, units and error bars, and the transition from raw intensity to absorbance is presented logically.
Instrument uncertainties are quoted and propagated into percentage uncertainties, demonstrating thoughtful consideration of measurement variability.
Error bars and absorbance computations (A=âlogâT) are correctly applied and support the analytical narrative.
weaknesses: Unit mismatches (sensor output in dB versus reported WÂ·mâ»Â²) and inconsistent significant-figure use undermine full precision.
Uncertainty propagation omits sensor specification uncertainty and combined standard uncertainty on absorbance.
No statistical analysis (regression slope, RÂ², Îµ value extraction) is presented, limiting analytical completeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs data communication is clear and generally precise, with appropriate uncertainty treatment and accurate processing. Significant omissions in unit consistency, complete uncertainty propagation and regression analysis keep this at the upper end of the 3â4 band bordering 5â6. A score of 5 acknowledges the strong foundation while highlighting the remaining precision and statistical analysis gaps.

strengths: The research question is described within a clear, specific real-world context (UV protection by window glass) and linked effectively to relevant theory (BeerâLambert law).
The methodology is laid out in a step-by-step procedure with an equipment list (including instrument tolerances) and a controlled-variables table that allows for reproducibility.
The hypothesis is coherently connected to background theory, and risk assessment and repeatability (trial repeats, sensor warm-up) are addressed.
weaknesses: Quantitative details (e.g. specific UV wavelength ranges, lamp irradiance values, inline equations) are omitted, reducing absolute precision.
Some operational details remain vague (exact lampâsensor distance, data-extraction process from Capstone traces).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While minor ambiguities in quantitative details prevent a flawless band-6 description, the studentâs work consistently meets the 5â6 band descriptors: the research question is embedded in a specific context, methodological considerations are fully explained and reproducible, and theoretical justification is strong. Awarding the full 6 reflects the high level of coherence and reproducibility achieved.

strengths: The conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis and justified through direct reference to the BeerâLambert law and the experimental data.
Key findings (linear relationship between absorbance and thickness) are restated and linked to theoretical expectations.
Relevant scientific context is invoked, situating results within accepted principles of UV attenuation by glass.
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative comparison with literature Îµ values or discussion of wavelength-dependent absorbance.
Broader practical implications (e.g. for glass manufacturing standards or device design) are not explored.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion meets the highest descriptor by providing a justified, coherent summary that aligns fully with the analysis and includes scientific context. Although quantitative literature comparisons are absent, the depth of justification and consistency with theory warrant the top band score of 6.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified (e.g. inter-sheet gaps, sensor warm-up variability, erratic trace noise).
Realistic and relevant improvements are described (use monolithic glass, increase trial count, extend warm-up time) and linked to each identified limitation.
The evaluation demonstrates reflective insight and practical problem-solving suggestions.
weaknesses: The discussion does not quantify the relative impact of each weakness on the final results (e.g. error-bar reduction estimation).
Improvements, while realistic, are not fully explained regarding their expected effect sizes or statistical benefit.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation goes beyond generic statements by describing specific limitations and corresponding improvements. It lacks only a quantified impact analysis to reach the very top band. A score of 5 recognizes the strong, targeted reflection and realistic recommendations, with room to deepen the explanation of impacts.

strandSelections.reasoning: **The research question is explicitly stated and embedded in a relevant real-world context** (UV protection offered by window glass; health risks of UVA). Background theory (BeerâLambert law, transmission/absorption definitions) is summarised and linked to the stated aim, giving the reader a clear, specific frame of reference.  Minor weaknesses (context remains somewhat generic â no quantitative citing of window standards, solar spectrum, etc.) prevent a flawless score, yet the description is still sufficiently specific and appropriate for the 5-6 band.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted, Â± values propagated to absolute and percentage uncertainties, and error bars are plotted. **However propagation stops there** (no combined uncertainty on absorbance; sensor specification uncertainty ignored) and some percentage calculations use rounded data.  This fits the descriptor âevidence â¦ with some significant omissionsâ (3-4 band, lower end).

Methodological considerations are presented (controlled-variable table, mention of sensor warm-up, trial repeats, risk assessment). **These considerations are described rather than fully explained**; the rationale for three trials, for using relative rather than absolute intensity, or for stacking versus single panes is not deeply explored.  Data sufficiency (range 0â7 mm) is reasonable but not justified.  Hence low end of 3-4 band.

Specific weaknesses are identified (stacking gaps, sensor warm-up, erratic traces) but **their relative impact on the final relationship is only hinted at**.  The strand therefore meets âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ without fully explaining impact, placing it in the 3-4 band, score 3.

The step-by-step procedure, equipment list (with instrument tolerances) and variable-control table **allow the investigation to be reproduced with only minor ambiguities** (e.g. exact sensorâlamp distance, lamp power specification, room light level). Repeat trials and stacking method are described.  Because some operational details remain vague (how averages were extracted from noisy Capstone traces, exact duration of each measurement), the work fits best in the 3-4 band.

Averages, transmission ratios and absorbance values are calculated and plotted; the linear trend is identified.  **Yet inconsistencies remain** (unit mismatch, no regression statistics, rounding errors).  Processing is therefore adequate but not fully accurate, meriting 3 within the 3-4 band.

Realistic improvements are suggested (use single-thickness panes, increase trial count, better sensor handling) and linked to corresponding weaknesses, satisfying âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ.  Thus band 3-4, score 3.

Tables include headings, units and uncertainties; graphs have labelled axes.  **Presentation is generally clear but not always precise** â intensity is reported in W mâ»Â² despite the sensor outputting dB; one table column mis-labelled; some figures poorly referenced.  Thus the communication is clear but precision lapses place it in the 3-4 band.

The student briefly compares results to the BeerâLambert prediction and notes that glass behaves as expected; this constitutes **some relevant comparison** but is not deeply explored (no literature Îµ values, no discussion of wavelength dependence).  Hence mid-band (3-4) with a score of 3.

The conclusion restates key findings and links them to the data and BeerâLambert law, making it **largely consistent with the analysis**.  While justification is present, it lacks quantitative discussion of gradient/Îµ and uncertainty impacts, so the work aligns with the upper part of the 3-4 band rather than 5-6.

annotations.comment: The introduction provides a clear real-world context by summarizing UV-A, UV-B, and UV-C distinctions and linking to glass protection, effectively framing the research question.
Although UV-A and UV-B distinctions are explained, the introduction lacks quantitative data on UV-A transmission through standard window glass or solar spectra, limiting specificity.
The transmission definition is clear but would benefit from an inline equation (e.g. $$T=I/I_0$$) and specified wavelength values to improve clarity for experimental measurement.
Consider adding a short example calculation or derivation following the absorption equation section to reinforce how percentage transmission converts to absorbance.
The aim statement clearly articulates the investigationâs goal but omits how proportionality will be quantitatively assessed (e.g. expected gradient or Îµ extraction).
The hypothesis is well linked to theory, explaining why increased glass thickness should attenuate UV light, demonstrating coherent scientific reasoning.
The materials list omits critical lamp specifications (wavelength, irradiance) necessary to reproduce intensity measurements accurately.
Specify the exact distance between the UV lamp and sensor (e.g. lab jack heights in millimetres) in the setup description to improve reproducibility.
The controlled-variables table clearly correlates each variable with its reasoning and control method, enhancing the studyâs methodological transparency.
The table entry for UV light power control lacks numerical wavelength or irradiance values; including these would reduce ambiguity in energy consistency.
Describing the dependent variable as ârelative intensityâ is vague; explicitly state how this metric is converted to absorbance in data processing.
The conclusion effectively links findings to the BeerâLambert law but lacks quantitative comparison with literature Îµ values, limiting scientific context.
Consider calibrating the Pasco sensorâs dB output to physical units (WÂ·mâ»Â²) before measurements to maintain consistent SI reporting.
Using range/2 for uncertainty is acceptable but neglects combined standard uncertainty; include propagation of sensor specification uncertainty for completeness.
Intensity units in Table 6 are given as WÂ·mâ»Â², yet the sensor outputs in dB; this mismatch undermines precision in data presentation.
Error bars and percentage uncertainties are presented clearly, demonstrating thoughtful consideration of measurement variability.
Include linear regression results (slope, $$R^2$$, and Îµ) on the absorbance vs. thickness plot to quantitatively confirm proportionality.
The absorbance computation using $$A=-\log(T)$$ is clearly presented and supports the transition from raw data to analytical results.
Absorbance values are rounded variably (e.g. three significant figures vs. two elsewhere); maintain consistent significant-figure rules across all results.
Extend the conclusion by discussing practical implications for glass manufacturing or UV protection design to situate results within broader scientific applications.
The evaluation accurately identifies gaps between stacked sheets as a limitation, demonstrating reflection on methodological weaknesses.
While limitations are described, the discussion does not quantify how these slit gaps or sensor warm-up uncertainties affect the numerical trend, reducing evaluative depth.
Recommend using monolithic glass of varying thickness instead of stacked slides to eliminate inter-sheet gaps; explain how this change would improve accuracy.
Suggest increasing the number of trials and extending sensor warm-up time to reduce random variability, and explain expected reduction in error bar size.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
